mission.title: Install and Configure Litmus on Kubernetes
mission.description: Litmus is an open-source Chaos Engineering platform that enables teams to identify weaknesses and potential outages in infrastructures by inducing chaos tests in a controlled manner. Installing Litmus allows SREs and developers to practice chaos engineering effectively in a cloud-native environment.

steps.title: Add the Litmus Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Litmus Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add litmuschaos https://litmuschaos.github.io/litmus-helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Litmus using Helm
steps.description:
Install Litmus in a new namespace called 'litmus' with the following command:
```bash
helm install litmus litmuschaos/litmus --namespace litmus --create-namespace --version 3.26.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Litmus pods are running correctly by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace litmus
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure that Litmus has adequate resources, edit the deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the chaos operator deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment litmus-operator --namespace litmus
``` 
In the editor, add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Access the Litmus dashboard
steps.description:
To access the Litmus dashboard, set up port forwarding with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/litmus-frontend --namespace litmus 8080:80
``` 
You can now access the dashboard at http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Litmus will show all Litmus pods in the 'litmus' namespace as running. You will also be able to access the Litmus dashboard through port forwarding.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add litmuschaos https://litmuschaos.github.io/litmus-helm-charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Litmus release from the cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources created by the installation. Run the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall litmus --namespace litmus
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. Execute the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd experiments.litmuschaos.io
kubectl delete crd chaosengines.litmuschaos.io
kubectl delete crd chaosresults.litmuschaos.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace litmus
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'litmus' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. After deletion, verify that the namespace has been removed. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace litmus
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Litmus installation. You can use the following command to export the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace litmus -o yaml > litmus-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Litmus release to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade litmus litmuschaos/litmus --namespace litmus --version 3.27.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the Litmus pods are running correctly with the new version. Execute the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace litmus
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Litmus pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs of the affected pod to diagnose the issue. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace litmus
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Litmus dashboard not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Litmus dashboard, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Verify the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace litmus
```
If the service is not running, check the deployment status and logs for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod eviction
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events in the namespace to identify the cause. Run:
```bash
kubectl get events --namespace litmus
```
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found when trying to uninstall or upgrade, verify the release name and namespace. List all Helm releases with:
```bash
helm list --namespace litmus
```
Ensure that 'litmus' is listed and check for any typos.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
